title: Kubernetes 101 — Fundamentals
description: Validate your understanding of the core Kubernetes concepts covered in this enablement: pods, services, kubectl, k3d, and k9s.
imagine: You just finished the Kubernetes 101 enablement in your Codespace. The TODO app is running on a local k3d cluster, monitored by Dynatrace, and you want to prove you actually understood how the pieces fit together.
yourGoal: Answer six questions about Kubernetes fundamentals as they apply to the codespaces lab you just completed. Each question relates directly to a tool or concept the enablement walked through.

story.introduction: The lab Codespace deployed a local k3d Kubernetes cluster with a TODO app and the Dynatrace operator. These questions reinforce what each component does.
story.context: Everything you need to answer can be found in the lab pages covering Codespaces and Content.

questions.title: What is k3d?
questions.content: The lab Codespace provisions a local Kubernetes cluster using k3d. What is k3d, exactly?

options.text: A managed Kubernetes service provided by Google Cloud
options.explanation: That would be GKE. k3d runs locally.

options.text: A wrapper that runs the lightweight k3s Kubernetes distribution inside Docker containers
options.explanation: Correct! k3d runs k3s (a stripped-down k8s) inside Docker, perfect for ephemeral environments like Codespaces.

options.text: A YAML linter for Kubernetes manifests
options.explanation: No — that's tools like kubeval or kube-linter.

options.text: A replacement for kubectl with a richer CLI
options.explanation: k9s is the richer CLI; k3d provisions the cluster.

questions.explanation: k3d packages k3s into Docker so you can spin up a fully-functional Kubernetes cluster on a laptop or a Codespace in seconds.
hints: Look at the Codespaces section of the lab — it explicitly mentions which lightweight k8s distribution is used.
k3s is a Rancher project; k3d is the Docker wrapper around it.

questions.title: Listing pods in a namespace
questions.content: Which kubectl command lists all pods running in the 'todoapp' namespace?

options.text: kubectl get pods -n todoapp
options.explanation: Correct! The -n flag selects a namespace.

options.text: kubectl list pods --namespace=todoapp
options.explanation: 'list' is not a kubectl verb — use 'get'.

options.text: kubectl describe pods todoapp
options.explanation: describe shows details of a single pod, not a list across a namespace.

options.text: kubectl pods --in todoapp
options.explanation: Not valid kubectl syntax.

questions.explanation: kubectl uses verb-noun syntax. 'get pods' lists pods; '-n <namespace>' scopes the query.

questions.title: Why use k9s?
questions.content: The lab pre-installs k9s alongside kubectl. What does k9s give you that raw kubectl does not?

options.text: It applies manifests faster than kubectl by skipping the API server
options.explanation: k9s still goes through the API server — nothing bypasses it.

options.text: A terminal-based interactive UI for navigating Kubernetes resources in real time
options.explanation: Correct! k9s renders pods, services, logs, and events as a live ncurses-style dashboard.

options.text: It replaces the cluster control plane during local development
options.explanation: k9s is purely a client-side viewer; it has no control-plane role.

options.text: It can run on machines without kubectl installed
options.explanation: k9s uses the same kubeconfig as kubectl and needs the cluster to be reachable.

questions.explanation: k9s wraps kubectl with a rich TUI, drastically speeding up exploration, log tailing, and pod debugging.
hints: Think about how the lab page describes navigating the cluster 'like butter'.

questions.title: Why expose the TODO app via a Service?
questions.content: The lab exposes the TODO app via a Kubernetes Service rather than connecting straight to its Pod. What is the main reason?

options.text: Services are required for any pod to start
options.explanation: Pods run independently of Services.

options.text: Pods get ephemeral IPs that change on restart; a Service gives a stable virtual IP and DNS name
options.explanation: Correct! Services decouple consumers from individual pod lifecycles.

options.text: Only Services can read environment variables
options.explanation: Pods read env vars directly from their spec or ConfigMaps/Secrets.

options.text: Services run faster than Pods
options.explanation: Services don't 'run' workloads — they route traffic to pods.

questions.explanation: A Kubernetes Service abstracts a dynamic set of Pods behind a stable ClusterIP/DNS, so clients don't break when pods are rescheduled.
hints: Think about what happens when a Pod is restarted.
What is unstable about a Pod that a Service fixes?

questions.title: Exposing a service from a Codespace
questions.content: The lab provides an `exposeTodoApp` helper that runs: `kubectl port-forward service/todoapp 8080:8080 -n todoapp --address="0.0.0.0"`. What does the `--address="0.0.0.0"` flag do?

options.text: Forces the port-forward to bind to all network interfaces, not just 127.0.0.1
options.explanation: Correct! Codespaces needs the forwarded port to be reachable from the VS Code host, which requires binding to 0.0.0.0.

options.text: Sends traffic through the cluster's external load balancer
options.explanation: port-forward never goes through a load balancer.

options.text: Encrypts the traffic between kubectl and the pod
options.explanation: kubectl port-forward already tunnels through the API server; the address flag is about bind interface.

options.text: Restricts access to the localhost loopback only
options.explanation: That's the opposite — 127.0.0.1 restricts to loopback; 0.0.0.0 opens it up.

questions.explanation: By default port-forward binds to 127.0.0.1. In Codespaces the VS Code port-forwarder lives outside the container's loopback, so you must bind to 0.0.0.0.
hints: Why is 127.0.0.1 not enough inside a Codespace?
The flag controls which interface kubectl listens on.

questions.title: How does Dynatrace observe the cluster?
questions.content: The lab installs the Dynatrace operator using a `DT_OPERATOR_TOKEN`. What does this operator deploy into the cluster?

options.text: Only a Grafana dashboard
options.explanation: Dynatrace doesn't deploy Grafana — it has its own platform.

options.text: OneAgent DaemonSet (and optionally ActiveGate) to capture metrics, traces and logs from the cluster
options.explanation: Correct! The operator manages the OneAgent DaemonSet so every node is instrumented.

options.text: A second Kubernetes API server for redundancy
options.explanation: Operators never deploy new API servers.

options.text: Just a sidecar container in the todoapp Pod
options.explanation: The Dynatrace operator manages cluster-wide instrumentation, not single-pod sidecars.

questions.explanation: The Dynatrace Operator reconciles a DynaKube CR and rolls out OneAgent (and optionally ActiveGate) so that every node, pod, and process is observed automatically.
hints: Operators encode operational knowledge as CRDs + controllers.
OneAgent is the data collector; what kind of workload deploys to every node?

questions.title: Verify Dynatrace discovered the astroshop namespace
questions.content:
Now that the Dynatrace Operator is running, use DQL to confirm that Dynatrace has discovered the **astroshop** namespace in your cluster.

Open **Notebooks** in Dynatrace and run this query first to explore what namespaces are visible:

```dql
fetch dt.entity.cloud_application_namespace
| fields entity.name
| limit 20
```

Then click **Validate** below to confirm `astroshop` is present.
questions.dql: fetch dt.entity.cloud_application_namespace | filter matchesPhrase(entity.name, "astroshop") | summarize count = count()
questions.explanation: Dynatrace discovered the astroshop namespace automatically via the OneAgent DaemonSet. The entity type `dt.entity.cloud_application_namespace` represents every Kubernetes namespace Dynatrace monitors.
hints: Navigate to Kubernetes in the Dynatrace menu to visually confirm namespaces are visible
The query uses matchesPhrase — both 'astroshop' and 'astroshop-load' will satisfy the check

learningObjectives: Explain what k3d provides and how it differs from kubectl and k9s
Use the correct kubectl syntax to inspect pods in a namespace
Describe why a Service is necessary even for a single-pod app
Understand how port-forwarding works inside a GitHub Codespace
Recognise how the Dynatrace Operator instruments a Kubernetes cluster
Use DQL to verify Kubernetes namespace discovery in Dynatrace
